title: Revisiting the SpaceX Valuation: A Post-Prospectus Update!
gist: Damodaran updates his pre-IPO SpaceX valuation after the May 20, 2026 prospectus, landing at $1.25-1.35 trillion equity value versus a rumored $1.8 trillion offer price ($135/share), and argues the AI business (via xAI/Colossus) is now both the biggest value driver and the biggest risk given Musk's supermajority voting control.

themes.theme: Prospectus data reveals a bigger, more levered, more concentrated company

insights.claim: SpaceX's prospectus, filed May 20, 2026, is 277 pages plus a 100-page addendum, which Damodaran describes as bloated with soaring narrative and pictures but containing 'weak links and multiple distractions,' consistent with his broader finding that IPO prospectuses have grown four to five times longer than decades ago without becoming more informative.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should not equate prospectus length or detail with informational value; the decision-relevant data is often buried in footnotes, not the bulk of the document.
insights.quote: prospectuses have become more bloated over time, often running four to five times longer than those filed by companies that went public three or four decades ago, but not necessarily more informative
insights.locator: The Prospectus: Data versus Information

insights.claim: SpaceX reported a 2025 operating loss of $2.57 billion (worse than Damodaran's pre-prospectus estimate of $2 billion) and, after nearly $2 billion in interest expense that was previously unknown, a net loss of about $5 billion.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The headline loss is materially larger than pre-IPO leaks suggested, and the debt load (unknown pre-prospectus) adds real interest expense drag even though it barely moves enterprise value given SpaceX's cash position.
insights.locator: The Prospectus: Data update

insights.claim: Because SpaceX's R&D spend is heavy, Damodaran capitalizes it rather than expensing it and estimates earnings before interest, taxes and R&D of $4 billion, implying the reported operating loss overstates how unprofitable the underlying businesses are.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts using GAAP operating losses at face value will misjudge SpaceX's true earnings power; capitalizing R&D is the correct lens for R&D-heavy young companies.
insights.locator: The Prospectus: Data update

insights.claim: The xAI acquisition pushed SpaceX's book equity to $41.3 billion (versus a pre-prospectus guess of $20 billion) and total debt including leases to $22.9 billion, but with $24.7 billion in cash the company actually has net debt of negative $1.9 billion (net cash), so the debt load has minimal impact on Damodaran's roughly $1.21 trillion enterprise value estimate.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Headlines about SpaceX's ~$23 billion in debt are misleading in isolation; net of cash the balance sheet is actually net-cash and immaterial to valuation at this enterprise value scale.
insights.locator: On the financing front

insights.claim: The prospectus disclosed 12,535 million basic shares outstanding, far above Damodaran's pre-prospectus backed-out estimate of 2,467 million shares, and this share count still excludes employee restricted stock units that remain undisclosed.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Per-share value estimates built on pre-prospectus share counts were off by roughly 5x; anyone using the earlier $1.2 trillion valuation to infer a per-share price would badly misprice the stock, and further dilution from undisclosed RSUs is still coming.
insights.locator: Share count

insights.claim: SpaceX plans to raise $75 billion in the offering and will hold proceeds in cash to fund infrastructure investment, meaning the cash boosts equity/firm value but not enterprise value.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Nearly all the increase in Damodaran's post-prospectus equity value (from ~$1.2T to $1.3T) comes mechanically from this cash infusion, not from improved operating fundamentals.
insights.locator: Use of proceeds

insights.claim: SpaceX will have two share classes at IPO: 6,932 million Class A shares (one vote each) sold to the public, and 5,602 million Class B shares (ten votes each) held entirely by Elon Musk, giving him control of more than 85% of voting rights.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Public shareholders will have essentially no governance leverage over capital allocation decisions, which matters directly for how much AI-spending risk they are implicitly underwriting.
insights.quote: with Elon Musk holding all of the class B shares, he will control more than 85% of the voting rights in the company
insights.locator: governance questions that will overhang the firm

themes.theme: Segment-by-segment story revisions: Starlink carries, AI overreaches

insights.claim: SpaceX's total 2025 revenue grew about a third year over year, but growth diverged sharply by segment: connectivity (Starlink) grew almost 50%, AI grew about 22%, and the space launch business grew only 7.64%, leading Damodaran to conclude that Starlink, not the AI or rocket business, actually carried the company in 2025.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The market narrative around SpaceX centers on rockets and AI, but the actual 2025 growth engine was Starlink connectivity, which should reframe where investors focus diligence.
insights.quote: it is Starlink that carried the company in 2025
insights.locator: Revenue Growth (Target Revenues)

insights.claim: xAI's Colossus compute center has been leased to Anthropic for $1.25 billion a month, which Damodaran expects to kickstart AI segment revenue next year, while also flagging future tension if xAI tries to compete directly against Anthropic in AI products.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A single counterparty (Anthropic) lease is a near-term revenue driver for xAI, but it simultaneously seeds a future competitive conflict of interest that could jeopardize that revenue stream once xAI pushes into Anthropic's own market.
insights.quote: which has been leased to Anthropic for an eye-popping $1.25 billion a month, which should kickstart revenues next year, with the potential of tension in future years if xAI plans to go head-to-head against Anthropic
insights.locator: Revenue Growth (Target Revenues)

insights.claim: The prospectus claims a $28 trillion total addressable market for SpaceX, with $26 trillion of that attributed to AI alone; Damodaran calls this 'borders on fantasy' and compares it to similarly inflated IPO TAM claims from Uber ($5.7 trillion) and Airbnb ($3.4 trillion).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Bankers' TAM slides in prospectuses are a recurring gamification pattern across major tech IPOs and should be heavily discounted, not used as a valuation anchor.
insights.quote: SpaceX has the largest TAM of any company in history, with a total TAM of $28 trillion, and AI accounts for $26 trillion of that market estimate
insights.locator: TAM by business

insights.claim: Despite rejecting the prospectus's $26 trillion AI TAM figure, Damodaran still doubled his own target AI revenue estimate from $80 billion to $160 billion, based on the Cursor acquisition and prospectus signals that xAI wants to seriously compete in enterprise AI, implying his own AI TAM estimate of $3-4 trillion.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Even a skeptical analyst materially raised AI revenue expectations post-prospectus, showing the AI segment is the single biggest swing factor in the updated valuation.
insights.locator: Story takeaway: I will stick with my estimates

insights.claim: SpaceX's space launch segment has the best unit economics of its three businesses with roughly 67% gross margins, reflecting cost advantages from reusable rocket technology, and Damodaran raised its target operating margin from 40% to 45%.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The core rocket business is quietly the highest-margin, most defensible part of SpaceX, even though it grew slowest in 2025 and gets the least market attention relative to AI.
insights.locator: The space business has the best unit economics

insights.claim: Starlink's gross margin improved from 37% in 2024 to 48% in 2025 and turned operating-income positive even before capitalizing R&D, but average revenue per subscriber fell from $99/month in 2024 to $66/month in Q1 2026 even as subscribers roughly doubled from 5 million (Q1 2025) to 10.3 million (Q1 2026).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Starlink is trading average revenue per user for volume/scale and it's working on margins, a classic land-grab pattern worth tracking via subscriber growth and ARPU trends each quarter; Damodaran held its target margin at 60%.
insights.locator: the revenues, per month, per subscriber, declined

insights.claim: SpaceX's AI segment has the lowest gross margins of its three businesses and those margins deteriorated further in 2025 due to intense LLM competition and rising delivery costs, leading Damodaran to cut his target AI operating margin from 45% to 25% even while roughly doubling target AI revenue.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The AI business is becoming a larger but structurally lower-margin part of the SpaceX story — a bigger, less profitable bet — which is the single most important net-negative revision in this update.
insights.locator: The AI business not only has the lowest gross margins

insights.claim: 2025 capital expenditures (~$14 billion) and R&D (~$9 billion) roughly doubled from 2024, with AI alone accounting for more than $14 billion of total reinvestment ($9.1 billion capex plus $5.1 billion R&D), which Damodaran calls 'breathtaking in its magnitude.'
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: AI capex intensity is now the dominant driver of SpaceX's total reinvestment need, meaning free cash flow generation is being pushed further out and value is increasingly contingent on AI capex paying off.
insights.locator: Reinvestment

insights.claim: Damodaran frames the AI capex surge as a bet with two possible readings: optimistically, it signals SpaceX/xAI intends to genuinely compete with Anthropic, Google and OpenAI in enterprise AI rather than settle for a niche; pessimistically, it locks in tens of billions more in near-term capex and risks major value destruction if xAI loses the AI competition.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The bull/bear case for SpaceX now hinges almost entirely on whether xAI can win share against three well-capitalized AI leaders, making competitive dynamics in enterprise AI a direct read-through to SpaceX equity value.
insights.locator: The positive twist that a SpaceX optimist would put

insights.claim: Post-prospectus, Damodaran's enterprise value estimate barely moved, edging from $1.21 trillion to $1.22 trillion, but equity value rose to about $1.3 trillion almost entirely because of the $75 billion IPO cash infusion, implying roughly $100/share, well below the reported offer price of $135/share (~$1.8 trillion valuation).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: There is a roughly 35-45% gap between Damodaran's fundamental value and the rumored/actual IPO pricing, flagging the offering as expensive relative to his DCF-based story.
insights.quote: The enterprise value for SpaceX edges up from $1.21 trillion, in my pre-prospectus valuation, to $1.22 trillion with the post-prospectus numbers, and the overall equity value increases to $1.3 trillion
insights.locator: With these updated inputs, I reestimate the cash flows

insights.claim: Damodaran raised his costs of capital used in the valuation (starting cost of capital from 8.02% to 8.37%, steady-state from 8.00% to 8.25%) to reflect the rise in US Treasury rates from 4.20% to 4.56% between his two valuations, noting both figures now sit near the median for all US companies.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A ~35bp move in discount rate is a mechanical valuation headwind independent of company fundamentals, illustrating how sensitive long-duration growth-story valuations are to the risk-free rate.
insights.locator: Since US treasury rates have risen

insights.claim: Damodaran's single biggest concern about SpaceX is that it will overreach in AI — starting with an overestimated AI target market and competitive position, and compounded by a voting structure that leaves Musk's Class B control unchecked even if shareholders want to rein in AI capex after it becomes clear the market is smaller or xAI's products are inferior to competitors.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The dual-class structure isn't just a governance footnote — it is the mechanism by which a misjudged AI bet could persist and compound rather than get corrected by market discipline, a key downside catalyst to monitor via capex trajectory.
insights.locator: the one that I would be concerned about the most

insights.claim: Damodaran flags the personal rivalry between Elon Musk and Sam Altman as a real financial risk, warning that it could escalate into what he calls a 'UFC match between two monstrous egos, funded by tens of billions of dollars shareholder money.'
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Personality-driven competitive escalation (not just market economics) could drive AI capex beyond what unit economics justify, a soft catalyst that's hard to model but worth watching in capex guidance and public statements.
insights.quote: you have the potential for a UFC match between two monstrous egos, funded by tens of billions of dollars shareholder money
insights.locator: antipathy that exists between Musk and Sam Altman

insights.claim: Damodaran states his overall SpaceX equity value is about $1.25-1.3 trillion (later refined to $1.25-1.35 trillion), and explicitly says he won't try to convince investors who value it at $3 trillion or half a trillion that they're wrong, framing disagreement over market size and profitability in young companies as natural and healthy.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Wide legitimate valuation dispersion ($0.5T-$3T+) for the same company signals this is a high-variance, story-driven stock where position sizing should account for genuine model uncertainty, not just point-estimate error.
insights.quote: I think that SpaceX is worth about $1.25-$1.3 trillion, but if you contend that it is worth $3 trillion or only half a trillion, it is neither my job nor my place to convince you
insights.locator: In short, based on my inputs and story

themes.theme: IPO mechanics: bankers add little value, pricing is anchored and discounted

insights.claim: Damodaran argues the SpaceX IPO exemplifies the 'dwindling value added by bankers on every dimension': banks can't time markets, filing mechanics are largely rote, pricing is really anchored off recent private-market transactions rather than banker judgment, and marketing/roadshows add little when social media and influencers already shape sentiment.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should discount the idea that bank involvement conveys informational or pricing quality signal for mega-cap, already-liquid private companies like SpaceX.
insights.quote: the SpaceX IPO is a testimonial to the dwindling value added by bankers on every dimension
insights.locator: The Bankers

insights.claim: SpaceX's pre-IPO private-market pricing was already about $1.2 trillion, and adding the $75 billion offering proceeds plus liquidity/S&P 500 inclusion benefits, Damodaran expected the offering to price between $1.5 and $2 trillion — and indeed a news report set the offer at $135/share, implying about $1.8 trillion.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: His pricing-range prediction (confirmed by the actual $135/share print) shows IPO pricing was driven mechanically by prior private valuation plus proceeds plus index-inclusion premium, not by fresh fundamental analysis.
insights.quote: there is a sense that the offering will be priced at between $1.5 trillion to $2 trillion, with or without the investment banking input
insights.locator: Pricing

insights.claim: Damodaran notes that IPO price guarantees are largely illusory because banks systematically set offer prices 15-20% below what they believe the market will actually pay, which is why he expects SpaceX's offer price to land below the top of its range — a discount partly cushioned because the float represents less than a tenth of total shares.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A deliberate 15-20% underpricing is a structural first-day 'pop' mechanism baked into IPO design — traders can systematically expect a discount-to-fair-value entry price relative to the top of the marketed range.
insights.quote: they systematically set offering prices at below (by 15-20%) what they believe the market will pay
insights.locator: Price guarantee

insights.claim: Damodaran points out that no investment bank has the balance sheet to provide meaningful post-market price support for a company priced near $2 trillion, meaning the traditional after-market stabilization safety net effectively does not exist for SpaceX if investor sentiment turns negative.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Downside volatility in the weeks after listing could be sharper and less cushioned than for typical IPOs, since the usual bank-backstop mechanism is scaled out at this size.
insights.quote: no investment bank has the capital to support the pricing of a two-trillion company, if investors turn negative on it
insights.locator: Post-market support

themes.theme: Life-cycle framework: why traditional value screens fail on young companies

insights.claim: Damodaran's core framework: for young, high-growth companies, value is driven by total addressable market sizing and unit economics found in prospectus footnotes, not by the current-period revenue/earnings figures in the financial statements themselves; as companies mature this shifts toward larger revenue, positive profits, and free cash flow available for dividends/buybacks.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors analyzing pre-profit growth companies should weight footnote disclosures on TAM, unit economics, and capital intensity far more heavily than headline income-statement figures.
insights.locator: Financial Statements and Value: The Life Cycle Effect

insights.claim: Damodaran argues that investors who screen out all money-losing companies or refuse to pay more than 20x earnings will end up holding only mature or declining businesses, and that this is a defensible strategy only if such investors stop complaining about missing high-growth stocks.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A direct challenge to traditional value-investing screens (low P/E, EV/EBITDA, profitability filters) as structurally unable to ever capture pre-profit compounders like SpaceX — the critique should be about mispriced fundamentals, not accounting losses per se.
insights.quote: Investors who are wedded to never buying money losing companies or never paying more than twenty times earnings for a stock will end up with portfolios of mature (and declining) businesses
insights.locator: For those who are focused on value metrics

insights.claim: Damodaran states that paying roughly 100x revenues for SpaceX (the level implied by a $1.8 trillion valuation) 'seems foolhardy' from a pure multiples perspective, even though he thinks dismissing SpaceX solely on the basis of losses, negative cash flow, or high revenue multiples is 'lazy and unconvincing' — the legitimate bear case rests on market-size and margin assumptions, or governance/key-man risk.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Separates a weak bear case (valuation-multiple sticker shock) from a strong one (TAM overestimation, margin compression, key-man/governance risk) — investors should build their SpaceX thesis on the latter, not the former.
insights.quote: paying a hundred times revenues for it (which is where a $1.8 trillion pricing would put it) seems foolhardy
insights.locator: SpaceX is a company with small revenues and large losses

themes.theme: Investor versus trader: positioning around the IPO pop

insights.claim: Damodaran distinguishes investors (who buy/sell based on a value gap and wait for it to close) from traders (who buy/sell based on expected price momentum regardless of fundamentals), and self-identifies as an investor who is 'not a very good trader.'
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A reusable lens for deciding whether to chase an IPO pop (trader logic) versus wait for price to converge to fundamental value (investor logic) — useful for any hyped mega-cap listing, not just SpaceX.
insights.locator: Investors and Traders

insights.claim: At a rumored $1.8 trillion valuation, Damodaran considers SpaceX too richly priced to buy given his own $1.25-1.35 trillion equity estimate, but says he would reconsider if the price fell enough, citing precedent that Facebook traded at half its IPO price within months and Uber lost over 50% of its market cap in the year after its IPO.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Historical precedent (Facebook, Uber) suggests high-profile IPOs often see 50%+ post-listing drawdowns, implying patience could offer a materially better entry point into SpaceX within the following year.
insights.quote: Facebook was selling at half its offering price a few months after its IPO, and that Uber lost more than 50% of its market cap in the year after its public offering
insights.locator: It is worth remembering that Facebook

insights.claim: Damodaran says he would not be surprised to see SpaceX's offering price at $1.8 trillion and then jump further in the days/weeks after listing, making entry at the offer price a plausible winning trade for momentum-driven traders, though he personally would not short the stock even if pricing rose toward $2 trillion, because mood and momentum are too unpredictable to bet against.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Even a bear on valuation explicitly avoids shorting an overpriced hype stock, underscoring that overvaluation alone is not sufficient reason to bet against momentum-driven names.
insights.locator: I would not be surprised in the least

insights.claim: Damodaran frames SpaceX overall as 'a loaded bet on the AI and Elon Musk,' noting some investors will draw confidence from Musk's Tesla track record while others will be deterred by the same personality dependence.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Frames the SpaceX investment decision as fundamentally a bet on two correlated factors (AI market outcomes and Musk execution/capital allocation), which investors should size and hedge as a single concentrated risk rather than diversified exposure.
insights.quote: this company is a loaded bet on the AI  and Elon Musk
insights.locator: A Loaded Bet on AI!
